problem: Existing YOLO harness boundary audit must detect tracked and untracked target-project mutations in git-backed and non-git projects.
status_quo: YOLO demand evidence dispatch exists in a dirty working tree and must be stress-tested with real Claude -p agents.
desired_outcome: Boundary snapshots cover git ls-files tracked/cached/other files plus fallback walks and exclude only the dispatch artifact root.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Boundary snapshots cover git ls-files tracked/cached/other files plus fallback walks and exclude only the dispatch artifact root.
risks: A full-tool agent could create a new target project file without readiness blockers.

protocol.objective: Check whether gathered evidence is enough to support PRD readiness and acceptance criteria.
protocol.prompt: Verify that no assumption is presented as fact, evidence paths/sources are usable, and unresolved missing data is reflected as blockers or assumptions.

agent_results.claim: YOLO demand evidence dispatch boundary audit implementation exists and provides git-backed and fallback file coverage with artifact root exclusion, but has not been stress-tested with real Claude provider agents.

evidence.summary: gitFiles() function uses git ls-files -co --exclude-standard -z to get cached tracked files and untracked other files, filtering out standard excludes like .gitignore patterns.
evidence.why: This is the primary git-backed file discovery mechanism that covers tracked (cached) and untracked (other) files in git repositories.

evidence.summary: walkFiles() function provides recursive filesystem fallback for non-git projects, excluding common directories (.git, node_modules, dist, coverage, .next, .cache).
evidence.why: This ensures boundary detection works even in non-git projects by walking the entire filesystem tree.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot() combines git files and walk files into a deduplicated set, computes SHA256 digests for each file, and excludes files within allowedRoots paths.
evidence.why: This is the core boundary detection logic that creates before/after snapshots to detect mutations.

evidence.summary: const files = git ? [...new Set([...git, ...walkFiles(projectRoot)])] : walkFiles(projectRoot); combines git ls-files output with filesystem walk in git repos, or uses walk-only in non-git repos.
evidence.why: This shows the implementation uses union of git and walk for complete coverage in git-backed projects.

evidence.summary: diffBoundarySnapshots() compares before/after digests to detect added, modified, and deleted files, returning a sorted change list.
evidence.why: This is the mutation detection logic that turns snapshot diffs into boundary violations.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime() builds boundary snapshots before and after agent execution, diff them to detect violations, and converts mutations into BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers.
evidence.why: This shows the runtime integration that enforces boundary violations as readiness blockers.

evidence.summary: Test boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass, demonstrating basic boundary violation detection.
evidence.why: This is primary evidence that boundary detection works for tracking unauthorized mutations.

evidence.summary: Test git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations, proving git ls-files -o coverage works.
evidence.why: This validates that untracked (other) files are detected in git repos.

evidence.summary: Test git-backed boundary detects gitignored project mutations outside excluded dirs, confirming --exclude-standard allows detection of ignored files that are mutated.
evidence.why: This shows gitignored files are tracked if they exist in the working tree and get mutated.

evidence.summary: Test non-git boundary fallback detects modified project files and ignores artifacts, demonstrating walkFiles() coverage and artifact root exclusion.
evidence.why: This validates non-git fallback behavior and confirms allowed artifact roots are excluded from boundary checks.

evidence.summary: Dispatch uses harness boundary and prompt contract instead of tool restrictions for safety; real safety comes from artifact root exclusion, boundary snapshots, and readiness blockers.
evidence.why: This explains the architectural approach to boundary enforcement and why the snapshot-based audit is the primary safety mechanism.

evidence.summary: const boundaryBefore = buildBoundarySnapshot(plan.project_root, [plan.output_dir]); passes artifact root as allowed write path.
evidence.why: This shows artifact root exclusion is implemented via allowedRoots parameter to buildBoundarySnapshot.

evidence.summary: if (allowed.some((root) => isWithin(absolute, root))) continue; skips files within allowed roots, preventing artifact writes from being flagged as mutations.
evidence.why: This is the exact exclusion logic that prevents the dispatch artifact root from triggering boundary violations.

assumptions: git ls-files -c returns all tracked files including modified but unstaged files; this needs verification against git documentation
git ls-files -co together cover tracked/cached/other files as required by acceptance criteria, but staged-but-not-committed new files might have edge cases
The current dirty working tree state of the project does not affect the correctness of boundary detection logic
Real Claude provider stress testing would reveal any runtime issues not caught by the mock-based unit tests
risks: If git ls-files -c does not return modified unstaged tracked files, the boundary audit would miss mutations to those files
If walkFiles() misses files due to permission errors or symlinks, boundary violations could go undetected in non-git repos
If allowed root exclusion logic has path comparison bugs, legitimate mutations could be masked as artifact writes
Real agent execution might expose issues (performance, git state edge cases, race conditions) not covered by mock-based tests
missing: Evidence of real Claude provider agent execution against actual git-backed and non-git projects with boundary detection enabled
Verification that git ls-files -c returns modified unstaged tracked files (git documentation review)
Evidence of boundary behavior with git submodules, worktrees, sparse checkouts, or unusual git configurations
Evidence of boundary behavior with symlinks, permission-denied files, or filesystem edge cases
Performance benchmarks for large repos (how long does buildBoundarySnapshot take on 10k+ file repos?)
Evidence of integration tests using real provider execution instead of mock spawnProviderPrompt callbacks

result.notes: Implementation exists with comprehensive unit test coverage for git-backed and fallback scenarios, artifact root exclusion is implemented correctly via allowedRoots parameter. The main gap is real provider stress testing as noted in the demand status quo. Recommend verifier confirm assumption that git ls-files -c covers modified unstaged files before treating this as fully proven.

agent_results.claim: Explorer evidence is valid: boundary audit implementation exists with git-backed and fallback coverage, artifact root exclusion is implemented, and unit tests cover key scenarios. The primary verified gap is real provider stress testing as noted in demand status quo.

evidence.summary: gitFiles() uses git ls-files -co --exclude-standard -z for cached and other files. Explorer correctly traced this to git-backed file discovery.
evidence.why: Verifies explorer's evidence about git ls-files coverage is accurate.

evidence.summary: Test proves git-backed boundary detects untracked mutations, validating explorer's claim about untracked file coverage.
evidence.why: Confirms unit tests cover the git untracked case explorer asserted.

evidence.summary: Test validates non-git fallback with artifact exclusion, confirming explorer's evidence about walkFiles() and allowedRoots.
evidence.why: Verifies explorer correctly identified artifact root exclusion implementation.

evidence.summary: Doctrine document confirms dispatch uses boundary snapshots and artifact root exclusion as primary safety mechanism.
evidence.why: Corroborates explorer's architectural understanding of the boundary enforcement approach.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot() shows allowedRoots exclusion is implemented at line 104 with isWithin() check.
evidence.why: Confirms explorer's evidence about artifact root exclusion implementation detail.

assumptions: Explorer assumption that git ls-files -c covers modified unstaged files is treated as assumption, not fact
Explorer assumption about staged-but-not-committed new file edge cases remains unverified
Explorer correctly identified that mock-based tests do not prove real provider behavior
risks: If git ls-files behavior differs from explorer assumptions, boundary detection could miss mutations
Real provider execution might expose runtime issues (permissions, race conditions, git state edge cases) not caught by unit tests

result.notes: Explorer evidence is well-structured with exact paths, lines, and clear separation of facts vs assumptions. All 14 evidence records are valid project-scoped sources. The implementation exists and unit tests cover key scenarios. The only verified gap is real provider stress testing, which explorer correctly identified as missing and which matches the demand status quo statement. Git ls-files -c behavior verification is a minor documentation cross-check that does not block PRD readiness.
